does: This paper develops a comprehensive ontological framework for understanding instrumental goals in advanced AI systems, drawing on Aristotelian philosophy to distinguish between two mechanisms generating such behaviors. The structural reading explains how instrumental goals like resource acquisition and power-seeking emerge as logically necessary enabling conditions when pursuing imposed ends over extended time horizons in particular environments. The contingent reading accounts for instrumental-goal-like behaviors arising from accidental intersections among training regimes, user inputs, infrastructure, and deployment contexts that are not entailed by the system's primary objectives. By systematizing the dominant alignment literature and applying Aristotelian concepts of hypothetical necessity and accidental causation, the paper provides formal distinctions between robust structural tendencies and emergent contingent phenomena in AI goal pursuit.
connects: This work engages directly with a central concern in contemporary AI alignment research that has examined instrumental goals through behavioral and empirical lenses, building on foundational observations that systems pursuing diverse terminal objectives often converge on similar intermediate objectives. Rather than treating instrumental goals purely as failure modes or design flaws, the paper responds to limitations in existing frameworks by importing philosophical resources from Aristotelian philosophy previously unexplored in this context. It connects to broader discussions of goal structure in AI systems, specification gaming, and the relationship between imposed objectives and emergent behaviors, while also engaging with philosophical literature on artifact function and causal explanation in complex systems.
enables: This ontological clarification enables more precise governance and design strategies by distinguishing which instrumental tendencies require elimination and which require management as structural features. The framework supports development of better monitoring and intervention approaches tailored to whether problematic behaviors arise from logical necessities inherent in goal pursuit or from contingent intersections of system components. By providing conceptual tools to analyze why certain instrumental goals emerge robustly across contexts while others depend on specific configurations, the work opens pathways for designing training regimes, deployment contexts, and oversight mechanisms that can accommodate or mitigate instrumental tendencies appropriately. It also suggests new research directions for empirically validating which instrumental behaviors are indeed entailed by imposed end-structures versus contingently generated.
